text: The library exists before you enter it. There is a peculiar gravity to serious archives, the way they warp the air outside their doors into something anticipatory, as though the accumulated weight of all those slow-deposited thoughts bends the afternoon light entering through tall windows into something more amber, more ancient than it has any right to be. I am thinking of the kind of place where even sound behaves differently, where a dropped pencil apologizes to the silence rather than disturbing it, where the dust on the upper shelves is not neglect but stratigraphy, each layer a year of someone's forgetting laid down like sediment. You arrive with your ordinary mind and something begins reorganizing you almost immediately, the way cold water reorganizes a fever, and you leave carrying ideas you cannot quite claim as your own, as though the building had thoughts and you simply happened to walk through them.
